title: Marriage Registration Basics in Sri Lanka
summary: A simple introduction to why marriage registration matters and what records couples should keep.
plainLanguage: In Sri Lanka, marriage registration matters because it creates an official record. That record is often important later for issues involving maintenance, inheritance, proof of marital status, and administrative procedures.

The Marriage Registration Ordinance is one of the main legal frameworks used for civil marriage registration. Different communities may also be governed by different legal frameworks, so the exact rules can depend on the people involved and the type of marriage.
For ordinary users, the safest practical step is to make sure the marriage is properly registered and that copies of the certificate are kept safely.
whatYouCanDo: Keep the original marriage certificate and at least one photocopy or scanned copy.
Check names, NIC numbers, and dates carefully on the certificate.
If you need proof later for bank, school, visa, or court matters, use the official certificate rather than informal letters.
If there is a problem with registration, contact the Registrar General or get legal advice early.
whenToContactLawyer: Contact a lawyer if the marriage was not properly registered, if there is a dispute about validity, or if you are dealing with divorce, maintenance, inheritance, or child-related court proceedings.
keyTakeaway: Registration creates proof. In many legal situations, proof matters as much as the story itself.

sources.title: Marriage Registration Ordinance - LawNet
Marriage Registration (Amendment) Act, No. 22 of 2013 - Parliament

That record is often important later for issues involving maintenance, inheritance, proof of marital status, and administrative procedures. The Marriage Registration Ordinance is one of the main legal frameworks used for civil marriage registration. Different communities may also be governed by different legal frameworks, so the exact rules can depend on the people involved and the type of marriage. For ordinary users, the safest practical step is to make sure the marriage is properly registered and that copies of the certificate are kept safely. Keep the original marriage certificate and at least one photocopy or scanned copy. Check names, NIC numbers, and dates carefully on the certificate. If you need proof later for bank, school, visa, or court matters, use the official certificate rather than informal letters. If there is a problem with registration, contact the Registrar General or get legal advice early. Contact a lawyer if the marriage was not properly registered, if there is a dispute about validity, or if you are dealing with divorce, maintenance, inheritance, or child-related court proceedings. Registration creates proof. In many legal situations, proof matters as much as the story itself.

title: Maintenance for Spouses and Children
summary: A plain-language guide to the idea of maintenance when a person with sufficient means neglects a spouse or child who needs support.
plainLanguage: Sri Lankan maintenance law allows courts to order support when a person with sufficient means neglects or refuses to maintain a spouse or child who is entitled to support.
The legal rules are court-based, so this is not something solved by private promises alone when a dispute becomes serious. The court can look at income, means, and circumstances.
For citizens, the main practical lesson is to keep evidence: expenses for food, school, medicine, rent, bank transfers, and any messages showing refusal to support.
whatYouCanDo: Keep records of household expenses and child-related costs.
Save bank slips, transfer proofs, and message history about support.
Write down the dates when support stopped or was reduced.
Visit Legal Aid or a lawyer if the refusal continues.
whenToContactLawyer: Contact a lawyer or Legal Aid if support has stopped, if a child is affected, or if you need help filing or responding to a maintenance case.
keyTakeaway: Maintenance cases depend heavily on documents, income details, and a clear record of neglect or refusal.

sources.title: Maintenance Act, No. 37 of 1999 - LawNet

The legal rules are court-based, so this is not something solved by private promises alone when a dispute becomes serious. The court can look at income, means, and circumstances. title: Protection Orders for Domestic Violence
summary: The law allows an aggrieved person to apply to the Magistrate’s Court for a Protection Order.
plainLanguage: The Prevention of Domestic Violence Act allows a person who has suffered, is suffering, or is likely to suffer domestic violence to apply to the Magistrate’s Court for a Protection Order.
The law also allows applications to be made on behalf of a child by certain responsible persons, and in some situations by a police officer on behalf of an aggrieved person.
If there is immediate danger, safety comes first. Court steps, police help, trusted relatives, shelters, and lawyer support may all become important very quickly.
whatYouCanDo: Go to a safe place first if there is immediate danger.
Keep messages, photos, medical records, or witness details if available.
Report urgent threats to the police.
Call relevant support services such as the women’s helpline and seek legal help for a Protection Order.
whenToContactLawyer: Get legal help as soon as possible if you need a Protection Order, if a child is involved, or if the violence is ongoing or escalating.
keyTakeaway: Domestic violence is not just a private problem. Sri Lankan law provides a court-based protection mechanism.

sources.title: Prevention of Domestic Violence Act, No. 34 of 2005 - Parliament
Ministry of Women and Child Affairs

title: Tenant Rights and Notice Before Leaving a Rental
summary: A practical guide for tenants who are suddenly told to vacate a property.
plainLanguage: Sri Lanka’s Rent Act is part of the legal background for many tenancy disputes. In real life, many people leave in panic when a landlord gives sudden verbal instructions to vacate.
A tenant should not assume that a verbal demand automatically settles the issue. Written records, receipts, and the exact nature of the tenancy matter a lot.
The safest approach is to avoid an emotional rush. Keep proof of payment, collect documents, and get legal advice when the matter looks serious or urgent.
whatYouCanDo: Keep rent receipts, bank records, and the rental agreement if one exists.
Write down the date and exact words used when you were told to leave.
Ask for written communication instead of relying only on phone calls or verbal threats.
Do not hand over keys or move out in haste before understanding your position.
whenToContactLawyer: Contact a lawyer quickly if you are being threatened with immediate eviction, if you have children or elderly family members at risk, or if the dispute is already becoming aggressive.
keyTakeaway: When housing is at risk, documents and calm action are better than panic.

sources.title: Rent Act, No. 7 of 1972 - LawNet

title: Eviction Basics Under the Rent Law Context
summary: An educational explanation of why eviction is a legal process and not just a private demand.
plainLanguage: Eviction disputes usually depend on the exact nature of the tenancy, the property, the evidence, and the legal route being used. For that reason, eviction should be understood as a legal process rather than a simple personal instruction.

If a landlord is trying to remove a tenant without clear legal steps, the tenant should gather records and get advice before acting. If a tenant has stopped paying or breached the agreement, that also changes the legal position and needs proper review.
This article is educational only. It is not a full statement of eviction law for every case.
whatYouCanDo: Collect the agreement, rent receipts, and messages immediately.
Take note of who is named in the agreement and who paid rent.
Avoid informal side deals made in anger or fear.
Seek legal advice before signing any document you do not fully understand.
whenToContactLawyer: Consult a lawyer if you have received a written legal notice, if the landlord is using force or threats, or if you need urgent advice on whether to stay or leave.
keyTakeaway: Eviction problems turn on facts and documents. The earlier you organize both, the better.

title: Rental Agreements, Receipts, and Messages: What to Keep
summary: The most useful property-law habit is keeping proof.
plainLanguage: Many rental disputes become difficult because people do not keep written proof. Even when the law can help, a weak record makes the case harder.
A written agreement, rent receipts, payment screenshots, utility records, and clear message history can all help explain what was agreed and what later went wrong.
This article is less about one section of law and more about building a safer position if a dispute starts.
whatYouCanDo: Keep signed copies of the agreement.
Save monthly rent receipts or transfer records.
Keep utility bills if they help show occupancy or payment arrangements.
Back up key messages by email or cloud storage.
whenToContactLawyer: Get legal advice if you are facing a claim about unpaid rent, damage, deposit deductions, or forced removal.
keyTakeaway: In property disputes, proof is protection.

title: Salary, Weekly Holiday, and Annual Leave Basics
summary: Official labour guidance explains weekly holidays, annual leave, and casual leave under the Shops and Office Employees law.
plainLanguage: The Department of Labour explains that workers covered by the Shops and Office Employees Act are entitled to weekly holiday arrangements, annual holidays, and casual leave according to the law.

For example, the Department states that in the second or any subsequent year of continuous employment, a person is entitled to fourteen days of annual holiday with full remuneration, subject to the law. The Department also explains casual leave rules.
If salary, leave, or holiday records are unclear, you should start by collecting pay slips, appointment letters, and attendance records.
whatYouCanDo: Keep salary slips and appointment letters.
Ask for leave records or attendance summaries in writing.
Send complaints or questions to your employer in writing where possible.
Approach the Department of Labour if your internal complaint is ignored.
whenToContactLawyer: Seek legal advice if large salary arrears are involved, if leave is being denied in a way that may lead to dismissal, or if the matter has already escalated into a formal dispute.
keyTakeaway: The official labour website is a good first stop, but your own records are just as important.

title: Maternity Leave Rights: The Basic Rules
summary: Sri Lanka’s labour authorities explain maternity leave under the relevant legal framework covering the employee.
plainLanguage: The Department of Labour explains maternity benefits under two common legal routes. Employees covered by the Maternity Benefits Ordinance are entitled to twelve weeks for a live childbirth, while employees covered by the Shops and Office Employees law are entitled to eighty-four working days for a live childbirth.
The exact entitlement depends on which legal framework applies to the employee. Because of that, workers should not guess. They should confirm which law covers their employment.
If an employer is refusing maternity leave or creating pressure around pregnancy, written records become very important.
whatYouCanDo: Ask HR or the employer which legal framework applies to your job.
Keep medical records and copies of leave requests.
Submit maternity leave requests in writing and keep proof.
Raise the matter with the Department of Labour if your entitlement is denied.
whenToContactLawyer: Contact a lawyer if you are being pushed out of work, denied a clear legal entitlement, or treated unfairly because of pregnancy.
keyTakeaway: The amount of maternity leave can depend on the law covering your employment, so verify that first.

The exact entitlement depends on which legal framework applies to the employee. Because of that, workers should not guess. They should confirm which law covers their employment. title: Termination of Employment: What Workers Should Know First
summary: Sri Lanka’s labour authority states that prior written consent of the employee or prior written approval of the Commissioner of Labour is required in certain termination situations.
plainLanguage: The Department of Labour explains that under section 2 of the Termination of Employment of Workmen Act, prior written consent of the employee or prior written approval of the Commissioner of Labour is required to terminate the services of an employee in the situations covered by that law.
This does not mean every job ending is identical. The facts, the category of worker, the reason for termination, and the legal framework all matter.
If you are told not to come to work anymore, or are pressured to resign, do not assume the situation is legally settled. Keep all letters, emails, and messages.
whatYouCanDo: Ask for the reason for termination in writing.
Do not sign resignation letters or settlement documents without understanding them.
Keep salary slips, warning letters, emails, and attendance records.
Contact the Department of Labour quickly if you believe the termination is unlawful.
whenToContactLawyer: Get legal advice quickly if you have been dismissed, forced to resign, or threatened with termination without proper process.
keyTakeaway: Termination disputes are time-sensitive. Early action is often better than later action.

title: EPF Basics for Private-Sector Workers
summary: A quick guide to the Employees’ Provident Fund and why workers should track it carefully.
plainLanguage: The Employees’ Provident Fund is a mandatory retirement-related contribution system for eligible private and semi-government sector employees who are not entitled to a government pension.

The Department of Labour explains that the employer contributes at least twelve percent of gross earnings and the employee contributes eight percent. Workers should not ignore EPF because missing or delayed contributions can affect long-term security.
Checking balances and ensuring correct remittances is a practical legal habit, not just a financial habit.
whatYouCanDo: Check your EPF balance through official channels.
Compare deductions on salary records with contribution records.
Raise contribution issues with the employer in writing first.
Report serious problems to the Department of Labour.
whenToContactLawyer: Get legal help if there is a large EPF loss, suspected fraud, or a prolonged refusal to correct missing contributions.
keyTakeaway: EPF problems are easier to solve when you notice them early.

sources.title: EPF Division - Department of Labour

title: Defective Goods: Refund, Repair, or Replacement
summary: A citizen-friendly guide to what to do when a product is faulty or not what was promised.
plainLanguage: The Consumer Affairs Authority states that it can inquire into complaints on goods as well as services. That means consumers are not left without a public complaint channel when a seller refuses to respond properly.
When a product is defective, your best first step is not anger but evidence. Keep the receipt, warranty, packaging, serial number, photos of the problem, and written communication with the seller.
Some disputes can be solved directly with the seller. Others should be escalated to the Consumer Affairs Authority.
whatYouCanDo: Take photos or videos of the defect.
Keep the receipt and warranty card.
Ask the seller in writing for a repair, replacement, or refund.
If the seller refuses, make a complaint to the Consumer Affairs Authority.
whenToContactLawyer: Contact a lawyer if the loss is large, if a business is acting dishonestly on a major transaction, or if the faulty product caused injury or major damage.
keyTakeaway: Consumer disputes are easier when your evidence is organized from day one.

title: How to Make a Consumer Complaint in Sri Lanka
summary: The basic steps for complaining to the Consumer Affairs Authority about goods or services.
plainLanguage: The Consumer Affairs Authority says it receives complaints on a wide range of products and services. The purpose is to help aggrieved consumers settle problems in the market place.
Before complaining, collect your documents carefully. A short, clear complaint with evidence is usually more useful than a long emotional story without proof.
If your issue involves both money and possible fraud, you may need more than one route, including police reporting.
whatYouCanDo: Prepare the receipt, invoice, screenshots, and seller contact details.
Write a short timeline of what happened.
State clearly what outcome you want: refund, replacement, repair, or other relief.
Submit the complaint through the Consumer Affairs Authority process.
whenToContactLawyer: Get legal advice if the dispute is high-value, repeated, or tied to a larger contract problem.
keyTakeaway: A strong complaint is clear, short, and backed by records.

sources.title: Consumer Affairs Authority - Consumer Complaints

title: Online Scams and Digital Fraud: What to Do First
summary: Sri Lanka Police warnings and practical first steps for victims of online fraud.
plainLanguage: Sri Lanka Police has warned the public about online crimes and internet-based financial frauds, including scams carried out through social media and online communication platforms.
A common mistake is waiting too long. If money has moved, speed matters. Contact your bank immediately, preserve screenshots, and report the incident through official channels.
Do not keep negotiating with scammers once you realize the situation is fraudulent.
whatYouCanDo: Stop payments and contact your bank immediately.
Save screenshots, account numbers, usernames, URLs, and call logs.
Report the matter to Sri Lanka Police and use the complaint systems available online.
Warn close family members if the scammer also has their details.
whenToContactLawyer: Contact a lawyer if the financial loss is high, if there is identity misuse, blackmail, or ongoing threats.
keyTakeaway: Fast reporting can be more useful than long arguments with the scammer.

sources.title: Sri Lanka Police - Public advised to remain vigilant and avoid becoming victims of online crimes

A common mistake is waiting too long. If money has moved, speed matters. Contact your bank immediately, preserve screenshots, and report the incident through official channels. Do not keep negotiating with scammers once you realize the situation is fraudulent. Stop payments and contact your bank immediately. Save screenshots, account numbers, usernames, URLs, and call logs. Report the matter to Sri Lanka Police and use the complaint systems available online. Warn close family members if the scammer also has their details. Contact a lawyer if the financial loss is high, if there is identity misuse, blackmail, or ongoing threats. Fast reporting can be more useful than long arguments with the scammer.

title: Your Rights When Arrested in Sri Lanka
summary: Article 13 of the Constitution protects against arbitrary arrest and guarantees important due-process rights.
plainLanguage: Article 13 of the Constitution says no person shall be arrested except according to procedure established by law, and any person arrested shall be informed of the reason for arrest.

The Constitution also says a person held in custody must be brought before the judge of the nearest competent court according to procedure established by law, and that a person charged with an offence is entitled to be heard in person or by an Attorney-at-Law at a fair trial.
These are fundamental protections. In real life, the safest response after arrest is to stay calm, avoid resistance, and ask for legal assistance immediately.
whatYouCanDo: Ask why you are being arrested.
Try to remember names, locations, and times.
Inform family or trusted contacts if possible.
Request access to a lawyer or Legal Aid as soon as possible.
whenToContactLawyer: Immediately. Arrest, detention, bail, and questioning can affect the entire case from the very beginning.
keyTakeaway: The first hours after arrest matter. Ask for legal help early.

The Constitution also says a person held in custody must be brought before the judge of the nearest competent court according to procedure established by law, and that a person charged with an offence is entitled to be heard in person or by an Attorney-at-Law at a fair trial. These are fundamental protections. In real life, the safest response after arrest is to stay calm, avoid resistance, and ask for legal assistance immediately. Ask why you are being arrested. title: How to File a Police Complaint and Keep Proof
summary: A practical guide to making a complaint and requesting extracts when needed.
plainLanguage: When you need to report a crime or serious incident, making a proper police complaint is often the first formal step. Your description should be clear, factual, and supported by available evidence.

Sri Lanka Police explains that the OIC of the police station can issue extracts of complaints made by the public on written application and payment according to law. For many users, keeping the complaint reference and later requesting lawful extracts can be important.
If a matter is urgent, emergency services and police hotlines should be used immediately.
whatYouCanDo: Prepare a short written note before you go to the station.
Take NIC, photos, screenshots, or witness details if relevant.
Ask for the complaint reference and keep it safely.
If needed later, ask about obtaining a lawful extract of the complaint.
whenToContactLawyer: Get legal advice if the police refuse to act, if the complaint concerns serious harm, or if you may also face legal risk from the same incident.
keyTakeaway: The complaint itself is important, but keeping the proof of complaint is also important.

For many users, keeping the complaint reference and later requesting lawful extracts can be important. If a matter is urgent, emergency services and police hotlines should be used immediately. Get legal advice if the police refuse to act, if the complaint concerns serious harm, or if you may also face legal risk from the same incident. The complaint itself is important, but keeping the proof of complaint is also important.

title: Bail Basics: A First Introduction
summary: A simple explanation of why bail is a court process and why early legal advice matters.
plainLanguage: Sri Lanka’s Bail Act provides the legal framework for release on bail and related matters. Bail should be understood as a legal process, not something that can be settled informally or guessed from rumor.
Whether bail is possible, when it can be asked for, and what conditions may apply depend on the offence, the court, and the stage of the case.
That is why families should avoid relying only on hearsay from bystanders. The better step is to speak to a lawyer quickly.
whatYouCanDo: Record the police station, time of arrest, and case details if available.
Contact a lawyer or Legal Aid as early as possible.
Keep NIC copies and relevant personal details ready for legal assistance.
Do not make payments to unofficial middlemen claiming they can “fix” bail.
whenToContactLawyer: Immediately after arrest or detention if bail may become an issue.
keyTakeaway: Bail is a legal route handled through law and court process, not rumor and panic.

sources.title: Bail Act, No. 30 of 1997 - LawNet
Legal Aid Commission - Ministry of Justice

searchText: Bail Basics: A First Introduction A simple explanation of why bail is a court process and why early legal advice matters. Sri Lanka’s Bail Act provides the legal framework for release on bail and related matters. Bail should be understood as a legal process, not something that can be settled informally or guessed from rumor. Whether bail is possible, when it can be asked for, and what conditions may apply depend on the offence, the court, and the stage of the case. That is why families should avoid relying only on hearsay from bystanders. The better step is to speak to a lawyer quickly. Record the police station, time of arrest, and case details if available. Contact a lawyer or Legal Aid as early as possible. Keep NIC copies and relevant personal details ready for legal assistance. Do not make payments to unofficial middlemen claiming they can “fix” bail. Immediately after arrest or detention if bail may become an issue. Bail is a legal route handled through law and court process, not rumor and panic.

title: Free Legal Aid: When and How It Can Help
summary: The Legal Aid Commission provides legal aid to deserving persons and has many centres island-wide.
plainLanguage: The Legal Aid Commission of Sri Lanka was established under Legal Aid Law No. 27 of 1978. The Ministry of Justice explains that the role of the Commission is to provide legal advice and free assistance of lawyers to low-income groups and to create awareness on legal procedures.

The Ministry also states that legal aid is given to deserving persons and notes the income guideline used in general, while also explaining that in special circumstances the income level may be relaxed, including certain maintenance-related matters.
This means citizens should not assume legal help is impossible just because they cannot afford a private lawyer immediately.
whatYouCanDo: Check the nearest Legal Aid centre.
Carry NIC, key documents, and a simple written summary of the problem.
Ask whether your issue falls within available legal aid services.
Keep copies of any complaint, police record, or court notice you already have.
whenToContactLawyer: Seek Legal Aid quickly if arrest, violence, maintenance, or urgent document issues are involved and you cannot afford private representation.
keyTakeaway: Lack of money should not automatically stop you from asking for legal help.

sources.title: Legal Aid Commission - Ministry of Justice
Government Information Center - Legal Aid Commission contacts

The Legal Aid Commission of Sri Lanka was established under Legal Aid Law No. 27 of 1978. The Ministry of Justice explains that the role of the Commission is to provide legal advice and free assistance of lawyers to low-income groups and to create awareness on legal procedures. The Ministry also states that legal aid is given to deserving persons and notes the income guideline used in general, while also explaining that in special circumstances the income level may be relaxed, including certain maintenance-related matters. This means citizens should not assume legal help is impossible just because they cannot afford a private lawyer immediately. Check the nearest Legal Aid centre. Carry NIC, key documents, and a simple written summary of the problem. Ask whether your issue falls within available legal aid services. Keep copies of any complaint, police record, or court notice you already have. Seek Legal Aid quickly if arrest, violence, maintenance, or urgent document issues are involved and you cannot afford private representation. Lack of money should not automatically stop you from asking for legal help.
